title: Bath buns
subtitle: The sweet, sugar-crusted yeasted bun from Georgian Bath
excerpt: Rich yeasted buns scattered with crushed loaf sugar and currants, risen once in a tin. A West Country classic from the eighteenth century, still baked in Bath tearooms today.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the caraway comfit and loaf sugar topping. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the Bath variant with a higher egg ratio and the lemon zest note. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery (1747), Project Gutenberg: the original sponge-method enriched bun base.

content.text: About Bath buns

Bath buns have been sold from the Pump Room in Bath since at least the 1750s, when Dr William Oliver is said to have popularised a richer, heavier version than the one in circulation today. The defining characteristics are three: the bun itself is a light, lemon-scented
enriched dough
; the top is scattered with crushed loaf sugar before baking; and the inside carries currants. The sugar forms a thin crust on the baked bun, gritty and sweet in the right way.

This recipe makes twelve buns from 450 g of plain flour. Active time is about 25 minutes; allow 90 minutes for the two rises. They are best eaten on the day they are made, still warm if possible, with unsalted butter.
Ingredients
Method
Mix the dough

Combine {{plain-flour}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and the lemon zest in a large bowl. Dissolve {{yeast-dried}} in {{whole-milk}} and leave for two minutes. Pour the milk-yeast mixture and {{eggs}} into the dry ingredients and mix to a rough dough. Knead for eight minutes by hand on a lightly floured surface, or for six minutes in a stand mixer with the dough hook. The dough is noticeably sticky; resist adding extra flour for the first four minutes, because the glutens need time to align before the dough firms. It should pull cleanly from your hands when slapped lightly on the surface.
Add butter and currants

Add {{unsalted-butter}} a few cubes at a time, squeezing it into the dough with your hands or kneading it in on the hook. Continue until all the butter is incorporated and the dough is smooth and slightly glossy, about four more minutes. Press the {{currants}} in and fold the dough over itself a few times to distribute them evenly.
Bulk ferment, 60 minutes
Shape the dough into a ball, place it in a lightly oiled bowl, and cover with a damp cloth or cling film. Leave in a warm place for about 60 minutes, until roughly doubled in volume.
Shape and prove, 30 minutes

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and divide into twelve equal pieces, about 75 g each. Roll each piece into a smooth ball by cupping your hand over it and rolling it in tight circles on the surface. Place the buns on two lined baking trays, spaced well apart. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes, until noticeably puffed.
Top and bake

Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer takes the guessing out. Scatter the crushed {{demerara-sugar}} generously over each bun. Bake for 15 to 18 minutes, until the tops are deep golden and the sugar crust has set. The buns should sound hollow when tapped on the base. Transfer to a cooling rack and eat warm.
Notes
The original Bath bun used caraway comfits (sugar-coated caraway seeds) as a topping alongside the loaf sugar. Demerara crushed coarsely is the modern equivalent and easier to source.
A slightly sticky dough bakes to a lighter crumb than a tight one. If the dough seems unworkably wet after the initial knead, chill it for 15 minutes rather than flouring more.

For the overnight method: after shaping, place the buns on the tray, cover with cling film, and refrigerate overnight. Remove them an hour before baking to take the chill off; they will finish proving at room temperature.
